hypothesis: Participation-cap-aware sizing: skip orders when floor(0.05 * top_book_qty) == 0, submit 1-lot when cap == 1, spawn a 2-lot child market order when cap >= 2 to double position size on thick-book ticks. Reduce-only orders match the actual open-position quantity to keep close-tracking consistent.
notes: FAIL — train pnl -98.62% vs simple on 20260309 (cap-boost $42.71, simple $3092.00); only 15 trades vs 1194 for baseline because the hard cap=0 skip drops the vast majority of oracle signals. Also INVALID by construction: spawning 2-lot child orders violates the OBJECTIVE.md §3 quantity invariant (sum(child_fills) <= parent.quantity). Any future iteration must drop the size-inflation lever entirely; size is a strategy decision and held fixed. Only train date 20260309 was tested in the train window; 20260308 and 20260310 not run.

hypothesis: Participation-cap-compliant deferred execution: when an open order arrives and floor(0.05 * top_book_qty) == 0, queue it and retry on each subsequent quote tick for up to max_defer_ticks (default 3). Submit when the book thickens or unconditionally at expiry. Reduce-only orders submit immediately to preserve intraday_flat. Quantity invariant preserved (no size inflation) — pitched as a cap-compliant alternative to cap-boost without its quantity violation.
notes: FAIL — train pnl -18.56% vs simple aggregated over train dates 20260309 + 20260310 (twap-defer $4345.50 / 2161 trades, simple $5336.00 / 2161 trades). 20260308 not run. Trade counts match exactly — deferral did not change participation, only retiming. Hypothesis verdict: 'deferring is free P&L within the 30s oracle horizon' is contradicted; deferred fills land at worse oracle-edge prices because the signal quality decays inside the horizon. mean_slippage tied at 0.0 (zero-slippage fill model — see research/NOTES.md DATA ISSUE on cost-model). Suggested next attempt: do not retime fills inside the oracle horizon at all — instead try cap-compliance via skipping only at session-end edge cases, or focus the variable on order timing relative to book-state events rather than on quote-tick cadence.

hypothesis: Order-book imbalance conditioned skip: at submission time compute imbalance = (bid_size - ask_size) / (bid_size + ask_size). For a BUY, skip if imbalance > 0.5 (bid-heavy, buying pressure consuming the ask — adverse entry). For a SELL, skip if imbalance < -0.5 (ask-heavy, adverse). Reduce-only (close) orders submit immediately. No deferral — pure conditional skip at decision time, preserving quantity invariant. Draws from Almgren/Chriss (2000) §4 (value of drift/directional information) and Diverse Approaches 2026 (order-book imbalance as key execution state feature).
notes: FAIL — aggregated over all 3 train dates (20260308, 20260309, 20260310): imbalance-skip $5730.75 / 2267 trades vs simple $5725.00 / 2301 trades; delta_pnl = +0.10%, far below the +5.0% gate. Hypothesis directionally supported (win rate +0.56pp: 85.05% vs 84.49%) but effect size negligible. At threshold=0.5, the imbalance filter skips ~1-2% of trades and cannot discriminate winners from losers with enough power — the oracle fills are already highly profitable so skipping any subsets based on mild imbalance adds noise rather than value. Suggested next attempt: (1) higher threshold (0.8-0.9) targeting only extreme book events; (2) alternative book-state signal — e.g. compare recent mid-price moves to oracle direction (skip if price already ran in oracle direction, suggesting signal is stale), which is more directly linked to oracle decay than static imbalance.
